title: Dog Whining and Pacing at Night: Causes and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog restless at night? Learn why dogs whine and pace, when it becomes a concern, and how to help your pet get a restful sleep.
content_html:
TL;DR: Nighttime restlessness in dogs is typically caused by anxiety, aging, or underlying discomfort and is rarely an immediate emergency unless accompanied by acute distress. Contact your veterinarian for a scheduled exam to determine the root cause.

Why is my dog pacing or restless at night?

It can be very distressing to see your pet pacing the halls or whining when the house is quiet. This behavior, often referred to as nocturnal restlessness, can be caused by a variety of factors:

  • Simple needs, such as a late-night bathroom break or environmental changes.
  • General anxiety or stress.
  • Cognitive dysfunction, frequently seen in senior dogs.
  • Hidden physical discomfort or chronic pain.

When is nighttime restlessness considered a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for this behavior is generally low. However, pacing and whining at night are considered immediate emergencies if they are accompanied by these acute symptoms:

  • A bloated or distended abdomen.
  • Heavy, persistent panting that will not stop.
  • An inability to stand or walk.

While most cases are not life-threatening, they indicate your dog is experiencing stress or discomfort that should be addressed during a scheduled veterinary appointment.

How can taking a video of my dog's behavior help the veterinarian?

Capturing a short video of your dog’s behavior is an invaluable tool for your veterinarian because:

  • Many dogs mask their symptoms or behave differently at the clinic due to adrenaline.
  • A home video allows the vet to observe exact behavioral patterns and posture in a natural environment.
  • Visual evidence helps the vet differentiate between behavioral anxiety, physical pain, and neurological concerns.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Whining and pacing at night in dogs can be associated with anxiety, frustration, or medical conditions. Behavioral signs may include restlessness, inability to settle, vocalization, housesoiling, and altered sleep cycles with interrupted sleep. Specific causes may involve goal frustration, such as the inability to access a stimulus, leading to displacement behaviors like stereotypic pacing and signs of anxiety. Metabolic or endocrine disorders should also be considered, as these can manifest as irritability, increased activity, and night waking. Additionally, cognitive dysfunction should be considered, as it can cause disorientation, confusion, altered activity levels, and changes in temperament, including fear, anxiety, or aggression, which can disrupt sleep.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1538)

faqs.question: Could my senior dog have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, Canine Cognitive Dysfunction (CCD) is a common cause for nighttime pacing and confusion in older dogs as their sleep-wake cycles become disrupted.

faqs.question: Could pain be causing my dog to pace?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Dogs often pace when they are unable to get comfortable due to joint pain, arthritis, or abdominal discomfort.

faqs.question: What can I do to help my dog sleep tonight?
faqs.answer: Ensure they have a comfortable, supportive bed and a quiet environment. If the behavior persists, consult your vet before trying any over-the-counter supplements.
